mitre_mapping.cti_sentence: The 'users' command is used to list all users currently logged into the system or on a networked device with high confidence.
The 'w' command is ambiguous without context as it could refer to a shell built-in or an external program like wget for downloading files from the internet.
The 'who' command is typically used to display the current user session information and can be associated with system or account enumeration techniques.
The sequence of commands 'users', followed by 'w' and then 'who' suggests a user enumeration process aimed at gathering information about the users on the system.
The earlier navigation into /var/log makes the later ls and cat auth.log commands part of a file and directory discovery sequence influenced by prior context.

The 'uname -a >> #{output_file}' command is used to display the system's hardware and software information about it in a human-readable format followed by appending this output into an external file for potential further analysis or evidence collection. This maps to data access with moderate confidence.

The 'if [ -f /etc/lsb-release ]; then cat /etc/lsb-release >> #{output_file}; fi' command is used to check for the existence of a file and if it exists, append its contents to an output file. This maps to data collection with very high confidence.

The 'if [ -f /etc/redhat-release ]; then cat /etc/redhat-release >> #{output_file}; fi' command is used to read the contents of a file that contains system information specific to Red Hat distributions and append it into an output file. This maps to credential access with very high confidence.

The 'if [ -f /etc/issue ]; then cat /etc/issue >> #{output_file}; fi' command is used to append the contents of '/etc/issue', which often contains system information or a custom greeting message for users. This maps to file and directory discovery with very high confidence.
